summary: Dr. Julie E. Buring discusses the complementary roles of observational studies and randomized clinical trials in epidemiology. She explains that when these study designs yield discrepant results, it rarely means one is 'wrong'; rather, it indicates the scientific process is working by revealing biological subtleties, highlighting different patient populations, or answering slightly different questions.

principles.statement: No single study can definitively answer a research question.
principles.rationale: Understanding a research question requires looking at the totality of evidence, which spans basic research for biological mechanisms, observational studies for real-world long-term data, and clinical trials for precise interventions.
principles.source_quote: assessing where we are and our knowledge about any question is going to require looking not at one study but at a totality of evidence

principles.statement: Discrepant results between study designs are a sign of the scientific system working.
principles.rationale: When observational studies and trials disagree, it forces researchers to look closer at methodologic and biological differences, ultimately advancing scientific knowledge and leading to better-designed future studies.
principles.source_quote: sometimes when there's discrepancies between observational studies and trials... there's nothing wrong at all that is exactly our system working our knowledge proceeding

principles.statement: Every study design has its specific niche and appropriate use case.
principles.rationale: Observational studies are ideal for long durations, big effects, or when trials are unethical. Randomized trials are ideal for minimizing bias to detect small-to-moderate effects.
principles.source_quote: every study has its Niche and for every Niche every question that we're trying to answer there is a study that is sort of the not necessarily the very best you can do but the appropriate one to start with

principles.rationale: To properly design a study and interpret unexpected results, epidemiologists must collaborate with basic scientists and clinical experts to understand the underlying biology.
principles.source_quote: it's not me alone as an epidemiologist or a clinical trialist but me working with basic scientists and then the clinical... expertise upfront to design the study in a way that will maximize my getting a true answer

frameworks.name: Investigating Discrepant Results
frameworks.when_to_apply: When observational studies and randomized clinical trials yield conflicting findings.
steps: Examine methodologic differences, such as uncontrolled confounding or compliance issues.
Look for biological differences between the study populations, such as age, weight, or timing of exposure.
Take the findings back to basic scientists to explore potential biological mechanisms that could explain the divergence.
Design subsequent studies to test the newly refined hypothesis.
frameworks.source_quote: you have to understand how the design of the trial could differ from the environment of the observational study in ways that could affect the outcomes under study whether it be patient characteristics agent characteristics the underlying biology

mental_models.name: Passing the Baton
mental_models.description: Viewing the scientific process as a relay race where observational studies identify promising hypotheses but are limited by confounding, and then pass the baton to clinical trials to tighten the variables and get a definitive answer.
mental_models.example: When observational studies showed vitamin E might prevent cardiovascular disease, the hypothesis was passed to the Women's Health Study trial to definitively test it by removing bias and confounding.

mental_models.name: The Totality of Evidence
mental_models.description: A holistic lens for evaluating scientific truth that requires integrating basic research (mechanisms), observational studies (real-world, long-term behavior), and randomized trials (controlled, precise interventions) rather than relying on any single study.
mental_models.example: Evaluating hormone replacement therapy by looking at basic biology (pro-inflammatory vs. favorable lipid effects over time), observational data (women initiating at menopause), and trial data (women initiating 10 years post-menopause).

heuristics: Observational studies give us a vague answer to real-life questions, while trials give us a precise answer to specific questions that may not reflect real life.
Don't rely solely on trials or solely on observational studies; you need what both can give you.
If a trial result is unexpected, look at the subgroups to see if biological differences (like age or gender) are driving the discrepancy.

signature_quotes.text: No one study can definitively answer a research question.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why the public and scientists must look at the totality of evidence.

signature_quotes.text: Observational studies really can give us the best albeit a little vague answer to real life questions while we always know we're going to be saddled with concerns and residual doubts about confounding.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the inherent strengths and limitations of observational studies.

signature_quotes.text: Trials will give us a precise answer by minimizing confounding but to very specific questions which may not in fact reflect real life.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the inherent strengths and limitations of randomized clinical trials.

signature_quotes.text: Sometimes when there's discrepancies between observational studies and trials... there's nothing wrong at all that is exactly our system working our knowledge proceeding.
signature_quotes.context: Reframing conflicting study results as a natural and positive part of scientific progress.

signature_quotes.text: All we want is to definitively answer the question either to clearly demonstrate a benefit or clearly demonstrate there's no benefit so that we can go on and use our scarce resources to look at something else.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the ultimate goal of conducting large-scale clinical trials.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming that when studies disagree, one of them must be fundamentally flawed or wrong.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Often, the studies are simply answering different questions or testing different biological contexts, such as starting hormone therapy at menopause versus 10 years later.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on randomized clinical trials as the 'gold standard' while ignoring observational data.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Trials are short-term and answer very specific questions that may not reflect real life, whereas observational studies can capture long-term effects and real-world behaviors.

anti_patterns.description: Treating preventive medication (like aspirin) as a replacement for healthy lifestyle choices.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Pills are just one weapon in cardiovascular protection and cannot replace the foundational benefits of diet, exercise, and smoking cessation.
